name: Big Horn River
short_description: Free rockhounding on BLM land along the Big Horn River. Hunt for agate, jasper, and chalcedony nodules in river gravels and eroded hillsides with minimal crowds.
description:
The Big Horn River area in northern Wyoming offers excellent public rockhounding opportunities on BLM-managed land. This riparian zone features gentle terrain at 3917 feet elevation, making it accessible for rockhounds of all skill levels. The river's constant erosion exposes colorful agate and jasper specimens in gravel bars and along the streambanks, while chalcedony pieces can be found weathering out of hillside exposures. The landscape combines open sagebrush benches with cottonwood-lined river bottoms, providing good visibility for spotting specimens.

The geology here reflects Wyoming's position in the Rocky Mountain region, where Tertiary volcanic and sedimentary rocks were shaped by millions of years of river erosion. Silica-rich groundwater percolating through these rock layers created the agate and jasper deposits that now weather out along the Big Horn River system. The river's constant scouring and sorting action concentrates these denser minerals in gravel bars, making them relatively easy to locate.

Expect to walk gentle slopes to reach the most productive areas near the river channel. Most specimens range from thumbnail to fist-sized, though larger pieces are occasionally found. The river corridor provides natural shade in summer, but the open sagebrush terrain can be exposed during spring and fall. Water access is available but always purify before drinking.
history: The Big Horn River valley has been mined and prospected for over 150 years, with early settlers recognizing the agate and jasper deposits in river gravels. The Tertiary volcanic deposits that created these silica-rich minerals date back 20-50 million years, when extensive volcanic activity across the Rocky Mountain region left behind the geothermal fluids that formed the colorful chalcedony varieties found here today.
tips: Focus your search in active and recent gravel bars along the river's inside curves where specimens accumulate naturally. Look for colorful banded agate and striped jasper pieces ranging from 1-4 inches in established lag deposits. Bring a small classifier or screen to process river gravels efficiently and work during low water periods (late summer through fall) when more gravel bars are exposed. The best specimens often show color variation from cream to rust to deep red, so examine pieces carefully before packing out your 25-pound daily limit.
terrain_notes: Gentle sloping terrain at 3917 feet elevation with open sagebrush and scattered cottonwoods. The river corridor features sand and gravel bars accessible by short walks (typically under half a mile) from pullout areas. Footing is uneven on gravel and rocky ground, but loose-fitting shoes can work for casual collecting. Spring runoff can make the river impassable in early season; late summer provides the safest and most productive conditions.
best_season: May - October. Peak conditions are July through September when water levels drop and maximum gravel bar exposure occurs. Spring (May-June) water levels can be high from snowmelt, reducing accessible collecting areas. Winter (November-March) freezing temperatures and snow may limit access on secondary roads.
written_directions: From Big Horn, Wyoming, take the Big Horn-Lovell Road (County Road 21) north toward the river valley approximately 8 miles. Look for BLM parking pullouts marked along the eastern side of the road where it parallels the Big Horn River. The main public access area is approximately 10 miles north of Big Horn town center. Park in established pullouts and walk down gentle slopes toward the river to access the best gravel bars and collecting zones.
road_conditions: Gravel county road suitable for passenger vehicles when dry. May become slippery during wet conditions or after rain. Summer conditions typically excellent.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts along County Road 21 where it parallels the river. Capacity varies from 2-6 vehicles per pullout. No facilities. Park well off the road to allow passage of other vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or equipment. No collection of vertebrate fossils. Pack out all trash. Leave no trace.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or power tools
No vertebrate fossils
Pack out all trash
Respect riparian habitat and wildlife
nearest_services: Big Horn, Wyoming (approximately 8 miles south) has gas and limited food services. Hardin, Montana (approximately 25 miles north) offers more complete services including grocery, restaurants, and lodging.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers. Uneven rocky and gravelly ground requires sturdy footwear but does not require technical climbing. River access involves short walks (typically under one mile) from parking areas. Some pullout areas are more accessible than others; scout several if mobility is a concern. Not wheelchair accessible due to uneven terrain and lack of developed trails or facilities.

faq.question: What types of rocks and gemstones can I find rockhounding at Big Horn River in Wyoming?
faq.answer: The Big Horn River area is known for beautiful banded agate, colorful jasper, chalcedony specimens, and quartz pieces. Most specimens range from thumbnail to fist-sized and accumulate in river gravel bars. You may also find petrified wood fragments, though agate and jasper are the most common finds.

faq.question: Is there a permit required for rockhounding at Big Horn River on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding on BLM land at Big Horn River. It is completely free to visit and collect. However, you must follow BLM rules: maximum 25 pounds per day, personal use only, and no commercial collection.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding near Big Horn, Wyoming?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season is May through October, with peak conditions from July through September when water levels are lowest and gravel bars are most exposed. Spring water levels can be high from snowmelt, and winter freezing may limit access on secondary roads.

faq.question: Are dogs allowed at Big Horn River rockhounding sites?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are allowed on BLM land at Big Horn River. Keep dogs under control to protect wildlife and respect other visitors. Bring plenty of water for your dog since you will be hiking away from your vehicle in a semi-arid environment.

faq.question: Is Big Horn River rockhounding suitable for families and kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Big Horn River is excellent for families and children age 6 and up. The terrain is easy, walks are short, and finding colorful agate and jasper specimens is rewarding for kids. The river corridor provides shade and water access, making it comfortable for a half-day family outing.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Big Horn River?
faq.answer: Bring a small pickax or rock hammer, garden trowel, and a bucket or bag for specimens. A screen or classifier helps sort river gravels efficiently. Wear sturdy hiking boots for uneven rocky terrain, bring plenty of water and sun protection, and consider a small backpack to carry your finds without exceeding the 25-pound daily limit.

meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony at Big Horn River on free BLM land. Easy terrain, no permit required, 25 lbs per day allowed.
